topic: reviewing latest periodontal literature — regenerative techniques

turns.content: I have been reading through several recent papers on regenerative periodontal techniques and I would like to discuss them, jan. The field is moving quickly and I want to make sure I am not falling behind.

I'd be glad to discuss the latest developments with you. Regenerative periodontics has seen some exciting advances in the past couple of years. What papers have caught your attention? Are you looking at specific areas like growth factors, scaffolding materials, or biologics?

A bit of everything, but I am particularly interested in the latest work on enamel matrix derivatives and their long-term outcomes. There was a systematic review published last month in the Journal of Clinical Periodontology that compared EMD outcomes at five and ten years post-treatment. The regeneration appears durable, which is encouraging.

EMD has been one of the more reliable biologics in the regenerative toolkit. The fact that you're seeing durable results at the 10-year mark is significant because early critics questioned whether the regenerated attachment would hold long-term. Did the review differentiate between types of defects — intrabony versus furcation involvement? The defect morphology usually matters a lot for EMD outcomes.

Yes, it did. The best outcomes were in narrow three-wall intrabony defects, which is consistent with what we have always known. Furcation results were more variable and the authors were appropriately cautious in their conclusions there. What interested me most was the combination protocol — EMD with a xenograft filler. The combination showed statistically better bone fill than EMD alone at the ten-year mark.

The combination approach makes biological sense — EMD provides the signaling for cell differentiation and attachment, while the xenograft acts as a scaffold to maintain space for the regenerating tissue. Without the scaffold, you sometimes get soft tissue collapse into the defect before bone has a chance to fill in. Are the xenografts they used primarily bovine-derived, or are you seeing more porcine options in the literature now?

The majority were bovine-derived — Bio-Oss continues to dominate the literature. But there was one interesting cohort using a porcine collagen matrix that showed comparable results with fewer inflammatory complications. I am watching that closely. The reduced immunogenic response is logical given the collagen processing techniques they described.

Porcine collagen matrices could be a game-changer if the data continues to hold up. Lower inflammation means better patient comfort and potentially fewer post-operative complications, which matters for patient compliance in the healing phase. Have you used any porcine-based products in your own practice, or are you still primarily working with bovine grafts?

I have used bovine xenografts almost exclusively. But I am open to evolving. That is the whole point of reading the literature — you cannot improve if you are attached to what you already know. I also read a compelling paper on platelet-rich fibrin as an adjunct. The simplicity of the PRF protocol is appealing — the patient's own blood, no additives, minimal processing.

PRF is really gaining traction and for good reason. The autologous nature removes any biocompatibility concerns entirely, and the growth factor release profile — slow and sustained over about 10-14 days — aligns well with the early stages of wound healing. The chair-side preparation is straightforward too. Some clinicians are combining PRF membranes with bone grafts and reporting accelerated soft tissue healing. Are you considering integrating PRF into your regenerative cases?

I am seriously considering it. The centrifuge is not a significant investment and the evidence base is growing. What gives me pause is the variability in PRF preparation protocols across the literature. Spin speed, spin time, the type of centrifuge — these all affect the quality of the fibrin membrane. I want a standardized protocol before I offer it to patients. I do not adopt techniques until I am confident I can execute them at the highest level.

That's the right approach. The lack of a universal PRF protocol is one of the legitimate criticisms in the field — Choukroun's original parameters differ from some of the newer low-speed protocols like A-PRF and i-PRF, and each produces a slightly different matrix density and growth factor concentration. If you do move forward, I'd suggest standardizing on one well-documented protocol and tracking your own outcomes rigorously. Your meticulous nature is actually a significant advantage here — clinicians who document carefully are the ones who generate meaningful clinical data.

That is exactly my intention. I track every case meticulously already — radiographic measurements, probing depths at baseline and follow-up, patient-reported outcomes. If I add PRF to my regenerative protocol, I will know within a year whether it improves my results. Thank you for this discussion, jan. It has helped me organize my thinking. The literature can feel overwhelming but talking through it clarifies what actually warrants adoption.

You're very welcome. Your systematic approach to evidence evaluation is exactly what responsible adoption of new techniques looks like. The field moves fast, but not everything that's new is better — and your instinct to demand rigor before changing your protocol protects your patients. When you do decide on a PRF protocol, documenting your outcomes from day one will put you in a strong position to contribute your own data back to the literature. That kind of practice-based evidence is invaluable.
